era_context: Shalmaneser III's reign in Assyria is in its final years (he died c. 811 BCE). The Levantine states remain under Assyrian tributary control. In Greece, the Archaic period continues with gradual development of the polis system and early colonization. Western Zhou in China has collapsed; the Spring and Autumn period is beginning. Egypt remains fragmented.

events.title: Shalmaneser III's final campaigns in the Levant
events.description: Shalmaneser III continued his military campaigns into the Levant, maintaining Assyrian tributary control over the region. These campaigns, documented in royal inscriptions, were among the final military operations of his long reign. His death around 811 BCE would mark the end of an era of sustained Assyrian expansion, though the empire's dominance in the Near East would continue under his successors.
events.certainty_note: Shalmaneser III's campaigns are well-documented; specific year assignment follows Assyrian chronology with ±2 year uncertainty. His death is dated to c. 811 BCE, making 815 BCE near the end of his reign.

events.title: Western Zhou collapse and transition to Spring and Autumn period
events.description: The Western Zhou dynasty in China was in its final years, with central authority fragmenting. The traditional date for the end of Western Zhou and the beginning of the Spring and Autumn period is 771 BCE, but the process of political fragmentation was underway in the early 9th century. By 815 BCE, the Zhou court's authority over regional lords was weakening, setting the stage for the decentralized political system that would characterize the Spring and Autumn period.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Bronze inscriptions and oracle bone records

events.certainty_note: The collapse of Western Zhou is traditionally dated to 771 BCE, but the process was gradual. 815 BCE is assigned here as part of the broader transition period, though no specific event of this year is documented.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events of 815 BCE are individually documented in surviving sources. The year is reconstructed from the broader pattern of Shalmaneser III's reign and the gradual collapse of Western Zhou. Chronological uncertainty in both Assyrian and Chinese dating systems means these assignments could be off by 1–3 years in alternative chronological frameworks. The significance of 815 BCE is largely constructed retrospectively from the broader historical patterns of the era.
historiographic_note: 815 BCE is not individually attested in surviving sources from any region. The year is assigned significance only through its position within broader historical patterns: Shalmaneser III's reign and the collapse of Western Zhou. This illustrates the fundamental limitation of year-by-year ancient history: most years leave no discrete historical trace. The surviving record is dominated by Assyrian royal inscriptions and later Chinese compilations; other regions and perspectives are essentially absent. African, American, and European history for this year is entirely unattested.

graph_edges.note: Shalmaneser III's death around 811 BCE marked the end of his reign and a transition in Assyrian leadership.
The gradual collapse of Western Zhou authority culminated in the traditional date of 771 BCE for the end of the dynasty.
